assessment.title: AI Engineer Onboarding Assessment
assessment.description: An adaptive placement assessment for AI Engineers. This bank contains 100 questions (10 per skill). During onboarding, 2 questions per skill are randomly selected to produce a unique 20-question assessment for each student. This is not a certification exam.

selection.note: Randomly draw 2 questions from each skill's 10-question pool at onboarding time to build a unique assessment per student.

assessment.instructions: Select one option for each question. The correct answer and its explanation are revealed only after you click an option. Answer honestly; this test places you on the roadmap and is not graded as a pass/fail exam.

questions.question: Which Python type stores an ordered, changeable collection of items?
questions.explanation: A list is an ordered, mutable collection.

questions.question: Which keyword defines a function in Python?
questions.explanation: def introduces a named function definition in Python.

questions.question: How do you store key-value pairs in Python?
questions.explanation: A dict stores data as key-value pairs.

questions.question: How do you use code from another module in Python?
questions.explanation: The import statement brings another module's code into your file.

questions.question: What is a Python virtual environment used for?
questions.explanation: A virtual environment isolates package dependencies per project.

questions.question: Which tool installs Python packages?
questions.explanation: pip is Python's package installer.

questions.question: Which is a valid Python string literal?
questions.explanation: Strings are wrapped in quotes, e.g. "hello".

questions.question: What does [x*2 for x in nums] produce?
options: A new list with each element doubled
questions.explanation: A list comprehension builds a new list by transforming each item.

questions.question: Which construct handles a runtime error gracefully?
questions.explanation: try/except catches and handles exceptions.

questions.question: What is the purpose of type hints like def f(x: int) -> int?
options: Document expected types and enable tooling checks
questions.explanation: Type hints document types and help linters/IDEs, without changing runtime behavior.

questions.question: In Java, code is primarily organized into...
questions.explanation: Java is class-based; code lives inside classes.

questions.question: Which method is the entry point of a Java program?
questions.explanation: public static void main(String[] args) is the entry point.

questions.question: Java is best described as a ... language.
questions.explanation: Java requires declared types at compile time (static typing).

questions.question: Which declares an integer variable in Java?
questions.explanation: int count = 5; declares a typed integer.

questions.question: Java code compiles to bytecode that runs on the...
questions.explanation: The JVM executes compiled Java bytecode, enabling portability.

questions.question: Which type holds text in Java?
questions.explanation: String represents text in Java.

questions.question: Which command compiles a .java file (classic JDK)?
questions.explanation: javac compiles source to bytecode; java runs it.

questions.question: Which Java interface represents a growable ordered list?
questions.explanation: List (e.g. ArrayList) is an ordered, index-based collection.

questions.question: Which OOP concept lets a subclass reuse a superclass's fields/methods?
questions.explanation: Inheritance lets a subclass extend and reuse a superclass.

questions.question: Which block handles exceptions in Java?
questions.explanation: try/catch handles exceptions in Java.

questions.question: What is the primary job of a backend server?
questions.explanation: The backend processes requests, applies logic, and returns responses/data.

questions.question: Why do backends expose APIs?
options: So clients can request data and actions in a defined way
questions.explanation: APIs give clients a defined contract to request data and trigger actions.

questions.question: Which is a Python backend framework?
questions.explanation: FastAPI is a Python web framework for building backends/APIs.

questions.question: What does backend 'routing' map?
options: A URL path to handler code
questions.explanation: Routing maps request URLs/methods to the functions that handle them.

questions.question: Where should backend secrets like DB passwords live?
questions.explanation: Secrets belong in environment variables or a secret manager, never in the client.

questions.question: The typical backend interaction is...
options: Client sends a request, server sends a response
questions.explanation: Clients send requests; the server returns responses.

questions.question: What is middleware in a backend?
options: Code that runs before/after handlers (e.g. auth, logging)
questions.explanation: Middleware wraps requests to add cross-cutting logic like auth or logging.

questions.question: Why are REST backends often designed to be stateless?
options: Each request carries what it needs, easing scaling
So they store all sessions in RAM
questions.explanation: Statelessness lets any server instance handle any request, aiding horizontal scaling.

questions.question: A common way to authenticate API requests is...
options: A bearer token (e.g. JWT) in the Authorization header
Putting the password in the URL
questions.explanation: Clients send a bearer token (often a JWT) in the Authorization header.

questions.question: Why add a cache in front of an expensive query?
options: Serve frequent results faster and reduce load
questions.explanation: Caching stores frequent results so repeat requests are faster and cheaper.

questions.question: Which SQL statement reads rows from a table?
questions.explanation: SELECT retrieves rows from tables.

questions.question: Which statement adds a new row?
questions.explanation: INSERT adds new rows to a table.

questions.question: Which clause filters rows by a condition?
questions.explanation: WHERE filters rows that meet a condition.

questions.question: Which statement changes existing rows?
questions.explanation: UPDATE modifies existing rows.

questions.question: What does a primary key guarantee?
questions.explanation: A primary key uniquely identifies each row in a table.

questions.question: Which clause sorts the result set?
questions.explanation: ORDER BY sorts rows by one or more columns.

questions.question: Which function counts rows?
questions.explanation: COUNT() returns the number of rows.

questions.question: What does an INNER JOIN return?
options: Rows with matching keys in both tables
All rows from the left table only
questions.explanation: INNER JOIN returns rows that have matching keys in both tables.

questions.question: GROUP BY is typically used to...
options: Aggregate rows into groups (e.g. count per category)
questions.explanation: GROUP BY groups rows so aggregates can be computed per group.

questions.question: Why add an index to a column?
options: Speed up lookups/filtering on that column
questions.explanation: Indexes speed up reads/filtering at the cost of some write overhead.

questions.question: Which HTTP method retrieves data?
questions.explanation: GET reads data without modifying it.

questions.question: Which method typically creates a resource?
questions.explanation: POST sends data to create a new resource.

questions.question: What does HTTP 200 mean?
questions.explanation: 200 OK indicates a successful request.

questions.question: What does HTTP 404 mean?
questions.explanation: 404 means the requested resource was not found.

questions.question: Which format is most common for REST request/response bodies?
questions.explanation: JSON is the dominant format for REST payloads.

questions.question: A REST endpoint is...
questions.explanation: An endpoint is a URL exposing a resource or operation.

questions.question: Which header commonly carries an API token?
questions.explanation: The Authorization header carries credentials like bearer tokens.

questions.question: HTTP 500 indicates...
questions.explanation: 500 signals an unexpected server-side error.

questions.question: Why version an API (e.g. /v1/)?
options: Change the API without breaking existing clients
questions.explanation: Versioning lets you evolve the API while old clients keep working.

questions.question: Why paginate a list endpoint returning many records?
options: Return manageable chunks instead of everything at once
questions.explanation: Pagination returns data in pages, improving performance and memory use.

questions.question: What is a Docker container?
options: A lightweight, isolated runtime for an app and its dependencies
questions.explanation: A container packages an app with its dependencies in an isolated runtime.

questions.question: What is a Docker image?
options: A read-only template used to create containers
questions.explanation: An image is an immutable template; containers are running instances of it.

questions.question: What does a Dockerfile define?
questions.explanation: A Dockerfile lists instructions to build an image.

questions.question: Which command builds an image from a Dockerfile?
questions.explanation: docker build creates an image from a Dockerfile.

questions.question: Which command starts a container from an image?
questions.explanation: docker run launches a container from an image.

questions.question: A key benefit of containers is that they...
questions.explanation: Containers behave consistently from dev to prod, reducing 'works on my machine'.

questions.question: Why publish a port with -p 8080:80?
options: Map a host port to the container's port
questions.explanation: Port mapping exposes a container port on the host.

questions.question: Why order Dockerfile steps from least to most frequently changing?
questions.explanation: Stable steps early let Docker reuse cached layers, speeding rebuilds.

questions.question: What is Docker Compose used for?
questions.explanation: Compose defines multi-service apps in one YAML file.

questions.question: Why use a Docker volume?
options: Persist data beyond a container's lifecycle
questions.explanation: Volumes persist data so it survives container restarts/removal.

questions.question: What does Amazon EC2 provide?
questions.explanation: EC2 provides resizable virtual machines (compute).

questions.question: What is Amazon S3 used for?
questions.explanation: S3 stores objects/files durably and at scale.

questions.question: An AWS 'region' is...
options: A geographic location hosting AWS data centers
questions.explanation: A region is a geographic area with multiple data centers.

questions.question: What does AWS IAM manage?
questions.explanation: IAM manages identities and their permissions.

questions.question: AWS Lambda lets you run...
questions.explanation: Lambda runs functions on demand without server management.

questions.question: A key cloud benefit is that resources can...
options: Scale up or down on demand
questions.explanation: Cloud resources scale elastically with demand.

questions.question: Amazon RDS is a managed service for...
questions.explanation: RDS provides managed relational databases (e.g. PostgreSQL, MySQL).

questions.question: The IAM principle of 'least privilege' means...
options: Grant only the permissions needed, nothing more
questions.explanation: Grant the minimum permissions required to reduce risk.

questions.question: Why deploy across multiple Availability Zones?
questions.explanation: Spreading across AZs protects against single-datacenter failures.

questions.question: A benefit of a managed service (e.g. RDS) is that AWS handles...
options: Patching, backups, and maintenance for you
questions.explanation: Managed services offload ops tasks like patching and backups.

questions.question: What does a vector database store and search?
questions.explanation: Vector DBs store embeddings and search by similarity.

questions.question: An embedding is...
options: A numeric vector representing meaning of data
questions.explanation: An embedding is a numeric vector capturing semantic meaning.

questions.question: Vector search finds items that are...
questions.explanation: It returns items whose vectors are closest (most similar).

questions.question: Vector databases are core to which AI pattern?
questions.explanation: RAG retrieves similar chunks from a vector DB to ground an LLM.

questions.question: Which is a vector database?
questions.explanation: Pinecone (also Weaviate, Chroma, pgvector) is a vector database.

questions.question: To query a vector DB, you first...
options: Embed the query into a vector
questions.explanation: The query is embedded, then compared against stored vectors.

questions.question: Embeddings have a fixed number of...
questions.explanation: Each embedding is a vector with a fixed dimensionality.

questions.question: Which metric commonly measures vector similarity?
questions.explanation: Cosine similarity measures the angle between vectors.

questions.question: Why use Approximate Nearest Neighbor (ANN) search?
options: Fast similarity search at large scale
questions.explanation: ANN trades a little accuracy for large speed gains at scale.

questions.question: Why attach metadata to stored vectors?
options: Filter results (e.g. by user or date) alongside similarity
questions.explanation: Metadata lets you filter/scope similarity results.

questions.question: 'Deploying' a model means...
options: Making it available to serve predictions/requests
questions.explanation: Deployment exposes the model so applications can use it.

questions.question: A common way to serve a model is behind...
questions.explanation: Models are often served via a REST/HTTP endpoint.

questions.question: Why package a model service in a container?
questions.explanation: Containers make model services portable and reproducible.

questions.question: 'Inference' refers to...
options: Using a trained model to make predictions
questions.explanation: Inference is running a trained model to produce outputs.

questions.question: After deployment you should monitor...
questions.explanation: Monitoring tracks performance and quality in production.

questions.question: To handle more traffic, a deployed model service can...
questions.explanation: Adding instances (horizontal scaling) handles higher load.

questions.question: Why version deployed models?
options: Roll back and compare model versions safely
questions.explanation: Model versioning enables safe rollbacks and A/B comparison.

questions.question: A user-facing model feels slow. What is a first thing to check/optimize?
questions.explanation: Latency depends on model size, batching, and hardware/accelerators.

questions.question: What is a canary deployment?
options: Release to a small subset before full rollout
questions.explanation: Canary sends a small share of traffic to the new version to catch issues early.

questions.question: 'Model drift' in production means...
options: Performance degrades as real data changes over time
questions.explanation: Drift is quality decay as live data diverges from training data.

questions.question: 'Scalability' is a system's ability to...
options: Handle growing load by adding resources
questions.explanation: Scalability is handling increased load, often by adding resources.

questions.question: What does a load balancer do?
questions.explanation: A load balancer spreads requests across servers to share load.

questions.question: In a typical system, the database is responsible for...
questions.explanation: The database stores and serves the application's data.

questions.question: A cache improves performance by...
options: Serving frequent data quickly from fast storage
questions.explanation: Caches keep hot data in fast storage to reduce latency and load.

questions.question: 'Horizontal scaling' means...
questions.explanation: Horizontal scaling adds more machines; vertical adds power to one.

questions.question: Stateless services are easier to scale because...
options: Any instance can handle any request
They store all data in RAM
questions.explanation: With no server-side session state, requests can hit any instance.

questions.question: A message queue helps by...
options: Decoupling producers and consumers of work
questions.explanation: Queues buffer work, decoupling services and smoothing spikes.

questions.question: Vertical scaling differs from horizontal scaling because it...
options: Adds power to a single machine rather than adding machines
questions.explanation: Vertical scaling upgrades one machine; horizontal adds more machines.

questions.question: How do you avoid a single point of failure?
questions.explanation: Redundancy ensures one component's failure doesn't take down the system.

questions.question: At a beginner level, the CAP theorem says a distributed store trades off among...
questions.explanation: CAP: under partitions you trade off consistency vs availability.
